thread.title: Gov. Little marks Idaho Water Day in celebration of new mitigation plan for farmers | Local News | postregister.com
thread.title_full: Gov. Little marks Idaho Water Day in celebration of new mitigation plan for farmers | Local News | postregister.com

author: DANIEL V. RAMIREZ dramirez@postregister.com
title: Gov. Little marks Idaho Water Day in celebration of new mitigation plan for farmers | Local News | postregister.com
text:
Idaho Gov. Brad Little, Lt. Gov. Scott Bedke, and Idaho Water Resource Board Chairman Jeff Raybould held a press conference Friday afternoon to celebrate the success of reaching agreements on statewide water mitigation plans.
On Oct. 28, Bedke and Raybould announced an agreement had been reached on the new mitigation plan. Under the new plan, it will be conducted in four-year blocks, with allotments of water given to each farmer in groundwater districts.
Monthly measurements will also be made to ensure accurate data collection. In the first two years, a storage allocation of 75,000 acre-feet for surface water users will be made, with discussions held in the third and fourth years to lower or raise the number.
GWDs will also be responsible for 205,000 acre-feet of recharge.
During the press conference Friday, Little signed a proclamation marking Nov. 22 as Idaho Water Day to celebrate the success of groundwater and surface water users collaborating to create a new mitigation plan.
Little said the new mitigation plan does two things for Idahoans. “It secures the agricultural economy going forward, and it gives us certainty.”
Bedke said while he is pleased with all the work that was done during the summer, he understands that it will take effort to ensure the new plan works.
“It’s going to be more than an annual basis; it’s every six months (in case) things come up; they’ve got to talk them out,” Bedke said. “There are going to be things, even as thorough as we thought we were being, (there’ll) be stuff we just missed.”
Over the summer months, Bedke and Little have met with stakeholders to discuss creating a new mitigation plan. Bedke said he is proud that politics was never mentioned during these meetings and that each party met at the table to talk openly.
“I never heard these four words: I never heard Republican, I never heard Democrat, I never heard liberal, I never heard conservative,” Bedke said. “Those guys reconciled themselves to the fact that they were stuck with each other and that they’re going to have to do it ... that’s when the magic started happening.”
Background In May, the Idaho Department of Water Resources issued a water curtailment order to farmers in eastern Idaho after a water delivery call notified them of a shortfall that would have affected senior water rights holder Twin Falls Canal Company.
The order curtailed 330,000 acres of farmland to recover 74,100 acre-feet of water that would’ve been short to the senior water right holder.
Under Idaho’s Constitution of Prior Appropriation, senior water rights holders must be protected from a shortfall, and juniors are forced to curtail.
On June 20, groundwater districts negotiated a temporary deal with the Surface Water Coalition, of which Twin Falls Canal Co. is a member. This temporary deal would allow farmers and those under the GWDs to continue irrigating their crops.
However, a new water agreement must be reached by Oct. 1. If no agreement is reached, the 2015 agreement will be reinstated for the 2025 season. This deadline was pushed back into November as a new deadline due to harvest delaying GWD members from meeting.
A new agreement was reached in late October, ensuring that new regulations will take effect for the 2025 growing season. This new agreement will also remove the 2015 agreement that caused issues in 2023 and 2024.
“We all never want to experience what the state did and what we personally did back in May and June of this year. We want to have something that everyone can get behind, even grudgingly so if we have to, but we do not want to go forward into a crop season without a mitigation plan,” Bedke said in October.
